question: What has roots as nobody sees, is taller than trees, up, up it goes and yet never grows?
hint: Think of something ancient and immovable that touches the sky yet was never planted.

question: I speak without a mouth and hear without ears. I have no body, but I come alive with the wind. What am I?
hint: It returns your own words to you, faithful but never original.

question: The more you take, the more you leave behind. What am I?
hint: Every departure creates them, yet they are made of absence as much as presence.

question: I have a mouth but never eat, I run but never walk, I have a bed but never sleep. What am I?
hint: It is both restless and patient, always moving yet never arriving.

question: I have hands but cannot clap, a face but no expression, and I tell the truth without speaking. What am I?
hint: It marks every moment of your life and asks nothing in return.

question: I have cities but no houses live there. I have mountains but no trees grow there. I have water but no fish swim there. What am I?
hint: The world in miniature — everything is present except life itself.

question: The more you have of it, the less you see. What am I?
hint: It is defined entirely by the absence of something else.

question: What gets wetter the more it dries?
hint: It sacrifices itself so that you may be clean.

question: I am always hungry and must always be fed. The finger I touch will soon turn red. What am I?
hint: It is the oldest servant of mankind, and the most treacherous.

question: I have a tail and a head yet I have no body. Kings have made me and beggars have spent me. What am I?
hint: Its two faces have decided more fates than any sword.

question: I have an eye but I cannot see. I help bind things together but I am not rope. What am I?
hint: The smaller it is, the more indispensable it becomes.

question: What is full of holes yet holds water faithfully?
hint: Its weakness is also its strength — the holes are the point.

question: The more of this there is, the less you see. It rolls in from the sea without being called. What am I?
hint: It obscures the world without malice — it simply is what it is.

question: It belongs to you but others use it more than you do. What is it?
hint: You were given it at birth, yet you rarely call upon it yourself.

question: Feed me and I live. Give me water and I die. What am I?
hint: It slumbers beneath ash, waiting for a breath to wake it.

question: I have no voice yet I can teach. I have no eyes yet I can show the way. I can live forever yet I can be destroyed in moments. What am I?
hint: The oldest living things are not trees but these — they carry the dead.

question: I follow you all day long but when night comes I am gone. What am I?
hint: It is loyal only so long as the light holds.

question: I show you yourself, yet I am not you. I show you truth, yet I cannot lie. What am I?
hint: It gives back exactly what it receives — no more, no less.

question: I fly without wings. I am counted but never touched. I heal all wounds yet I cannot be purchased. What am I?
hint: The one thing Vizzini himself cannot outwit — and neither can you.

question: I move through the trees without hands and through the sails without arms. I am felt but never seen. What am I?
hint: Invisible, it shapes everything — the ocean, the flame, the course of ships.

question: I have no legs but I travel farther than any horse. I have no wings but I can circle the world. What am I?
hint: Nothing outpaces it — not even thought comes close.

question: I begin at the end and end at the beginning. What am I?
hint: It is the only appointment that cannot be postponed, cancelled, or delegated.

question: I am taken from a mine and shut up in a wooden case, from which I am never released, and yet I am used by almost every person. What am I?
hint: Its heart is dark but its purpose is to illuminate.

question: I am always in front of you but can never be seen. What am I?
hint: Every step you take is toward it, yet it retreats at the same speed.

question: The more you share me, the more of me you keep. The less you share me, the less of me you have. What am I?
hint: It grows by giving — a paradox Vizzini would appreciate and never practice.

question: I have teeth but cannot bite. I have a spine but cannot stand. What am I?
hint: It brings order to chaos, one strand at a time.

question: I can run but never walk, have a mouth but never talk, have a head but never weep, have a bed but never sleep. What am I?
hint: Smaller than a river, but equally restless — always moving toward the sea.

question: What breaks but never falls, and falls but never breaks?
hint: One is the end of night; one is the end of day. Each has its hour.

question: I have a ring but no finger. I have a tower but no castle. I have a note but no music. What am I?
hint: It carries a voice across impossible distances, as if by magic.

question: You see me once in June, twice in November, but not at all in May. What am I?
hint: Look not at the meaning of the months but at their forms.

question: I have two hands and no arms, one face and no head. I mark every moment but I have never lived. What am I?
hint: Smaller than a clock but no less faithful — it travels with you.

question: I am the beginning of everything, the end of everywhere. I am the beginning of eternity, the end of time and space. What am I?
hint: Seek me not in meaning but in spelling.

question: What has one eye but cannot see?
hint: At its calmest centre, everything around it rages.

question: The more you remove from me, the bigger I become. What am I?
hint: It is made entirely of absence, yet absence is a thing.

question: I am light as a feather, yet even the strongest person cannot hold me for more than a few minutes. What am I?
hint: You cannot live without it, yet you rarely think about it until it is gone.

question: What has a neck but no head?
hint: It holds what it is given but never drinks.

question: I go up but never come down. What am I?
hint: It is the one thing Vizzini resents and cannot argue with.

question: What comes once in a minute, twice in a moment, but never in a thousand years?
hint: It is not a concept but a shape.

question: I travel around the world but stay in one corner. What am I?
hint: It is small, sticky, and represents a journey it will never complete itself.

question: I have no wings but I fly, no sword but I can cut through stone given enough time. What am I?
hint: The gentlest force in nature is also the most relentless.

question: I am bought by the yard but worn by the foot. What am I?
hint: Every hall in every castle has one — even in Florin.

question: What has a single horn and gives milk?
hint: Remove the fantastical part of the answer and only the useful part remains.

question: I can be caught but not thrown. The faster you run, the faster I follow. What am I?
hint: It is not kind to leave unattended in the night air.

question: I am white when I am dirty and black when I am clean. What am I?
hint: It holds what is written on it and yields it up when wiped.

question: I weigh nothing but even the strongest man cannot hold me for long. What am I?
hint: Vizzini has many of these. He is not good at keeping them.

question: What can you hold in your right hand but not in your left?
hint: The answer is in the question — you are simply not reading carefully enough.

question: I have three feet but I cannot walk. What am I?
hint: It measures the world without ever moving through it.

question: I am not alive but I grow. I have no lungs but I need air. I have no mouth but water kills me. What am I?
hint: It has served more vigils and banquets than any servant in history.

question: Cut me and I will not bleed. Break me and I give you more of myself. What am I?
hint: It is what you find in the moment before an answer. Or an execution.

question: I am full in summer, empty in winter, but I never hold water. What am I?
hint: It stands in every season, but its appearance changes entirely with time.

question: What goes up when rain comes down?
hint: It exists only in the relationship between sky and shelter.

question: I have keys but no locks, space but no room, and you can enter but cannot go inside. What am I?
hint: It unlocks expression but holds no physical secrets.

question: I sleep in the day and scream in the night. I have one eye and make many. What am I?
hint: It is wiser than its expression suggests. Or so it claims.

question: What has four legs in the morning, two at noon, and three at night?
hint: The Sphinx asked this question. The answer is always among those answering.

question: I have hundreds of limbs but cannot walk. I have a crown but no kingdom. I fall without moving. What am I?
hint: It asks nothing of the world and gives everything to it.

question: I protect like a knight but I am made of nothing. I am real only when threatened. What am I?
hint: It does not exist until you need it. And then it either is there — or it is not.

question: What is in your pocket that you cannot give to another?
hint: It is yours entirely — and yet you cannot keep it forever.

question: I can be broken without being touched. I can be kept without being held. What am I?
hint: Vizzini broke many. A man of principle keeps one.

question: I have a golden head and a silver tail. I fly straight and true. What am I?
hint: It is made for a single purpose and it fulfils it with terrible honesty.

question: The more you stir me, the thicker I get. What am I?
hint: It traps those who hurry through it and rewards those who step carefully.

question: I am always present in a court but never in a forest. Always in a battle but never in a duel. What am I?
hint: Spell it carefully.

question: What begins with a T, ends with a T, and has T in it?
hint: It is full of something warm and civilised.

question: I run without feet over mountains and plain. I fly without wings through the sky and the rain. What am I?
hint: It goes where it is told and never asks where it is going.

question: You must keep it after giving it. What is it?
hint: A man of honour gives one and a man of dishonour pretends to.

question: I can't be seen, touched, or heard, but I am always changing. I am behind every decision and beneath every fear. What am I?
hint: Vizzini believes his are extraordinary. He is not entirely wrong.

question: What can fill a room but takes up no space?
hint: It costs nothing and is worth everything on a cold night.

question: I am found in the sea and on the land but do not walk or swim. I travel by foot but have no legs. What am I?
hint: It has preserved kings and ruined meals in equal measure.

question: I have no beginning and no end, yet I am the oldest shape in the world. What am I?
hint: Every wedding band, every moon, every coin shares this form.

question: What belongs to you but everyone else uses it more?
hint: You build it over a lifetime and can lose it in a moment — as Vizzini discovered.

question: I can build a castle or tear one down, cause laughter or bring tears. I cost nothing to give but can mean everything to receive. What am I?
hint: Vizzini wields many of them. Not all to good effect.

question: What runs all day and night but never moves?
hint: It flows where it is and goes nowhere.

question: I grow older with time but stay fresh in the mind. I am gone the moment I happen. What am I?
hint: The present tense. You are always in it and never able to hold it.

question: The more you take away from me, the larger I become. Take nothing, and I am zero. What am I?
hint: Kings have fallen for want of understanding this.

question: I have a shoulder and no arms, a body and no legs, a foot and no toes. What am I?
hint: Look at the anatomy of landscape rather than of living things.

question: I have a bark but I am not a dog. I have leaves but no flowers. I stand tall but cannot walk. What am I?
hint: It has stood longer than most empires.

question: What do you throw out when you want to use it and take back in when you don't?
hint: It is the heaviest form of staying put.

question: I can travel the world without moving from my place. What am I?
hint: Every sky in every land carries it.

question: The poor have me and the rich need me. If you eat me you die. What am I?
hint: Think of what the destitute are never without, and what the wealthy never have enough of.

question: I am not alive but I can die. I cannot see but I can blind. What am I?
hint: Even the finest sword is not immune to time and neglect.

question: What gets stronger the more you use it?
hint: Virtue and vice grow by the same mechanism.

question: I am always confused with my twin but I am my own thing entirely. What am I?
hint: It and its opposite are often mistaken for one another at a glance.

question: I have no weight, no colour, no form — yet every great man has carried me. What am I?
hint: Leadership is inseparable from it.

question: I am born in the morning, die at night, yet am born again tomorrow unchanged. What am I?
hint: It is the most reliable thing in the world, and still it passes.

question: I have a bottom at the top. What am I?
hint: Anatomy, if considered from an unusual angle.

question: I have one foot on the ground and one in the sky and I grow the more I fall. What am I?
hint: Its fall nourishes what its absence would destroy.

question: What falls but never breaks, and breaks but never falls?
hint: One thing falls reliably every evening. Another breaks every morning.

question: I am a word of letters three. Add two and fewer there will be. What am I?
hint: The answer contains its own logic.

question: What can run but never walks, has a mouth but never talks, has a head but never weeps, has a bed but never sleeps?
hint: More dramatic than a river, more relentless than rain.

question: I am a path between high walls, open to the sky. What am I?
hint: Those who know cities know me. Those who know danger know me better.

question: I turn everything around yet stay still. What am I?
hint: Left becomes right. East becomes west. The world reversed without moving.

question: I am heavier than gold but lighter than a feather. What am I?
hint: It has no weight and yet those who carry it stoop under it.

question: I am a green house inside a white house inside a red house inside a brown house. What am I?
hint: It is made of layers, like a mystery — or like Vizzini's plans.

question: What has one thumb and four fingers but is not alive?
hint: It serves the hand that wears it and is useless without one.

question: I grow in water and die when wet. What am I?
hint: Structure emerges slowly from stillness — destroy it with the wrong touch.

question: What can travel around the world and yet stay in one corner?
hint: Small, sticky, and possessed of remarkable ambition.

question: I have a spine but no bones, chapters but no verses, a cover but no roof. What am I?
hint: It holds more truth than a history and more lies than a courtier.

question: I can fly without wings and swim without fins. I can see without eyes and speak without a tongue. What am I?
hint: It visits everyone and answers to no one.

question: I have a surface but no depth. I show truth but I am not real. What am I?
hint: The finest ones make you forget you are looking at pigment and cloth.

question: I grow downward when everything else grows up. What am I?
hint: The unseen foundation. Every tall thing has them.

question: What is harder to catch the faster you run?
hint: Exertion undoes it; stillness restores it.

question: I rise with wealth and fall with wisdom. What am I?
hint: Vizzini had abundant quantities of it. It did not serve him well.
